message:
feat(ftui): add command execution handoff state machine (FTUI-06.2)

Create command_handoff.rs implementing deterministic terminal handoff:
- execute(session, command) -> Result<CommandResult, HandoffError>
- State machine: Active -> suspend -> run cmd -> wait Enter -> resume -> Active
- Output gate integration via TerminalSession suspend/resume
- HandoffError variants: SuspendFailed, ResumeFailed, EmptyCommand
- CommandResult with exit status and launch error tracking
- Failure-path handling: resume errors leave session in Suspended (recoverable)
- 6 unit tests covering error paths and phase transitions

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-3gsu","title":"FTUI-03.2.b Route subprocess output through PTY capture and sanitized forwarding","description":"## Background
Subprocess output can violate ownership/order guarantees unless it is normalized before presentation.

## Deliverables
- subprocess forwarding contract (sanitization, ordering, backpressure, redaction boundaries)
- implementation/test plan for integrating subprocess output into the one-writer sink
- diagnostics for dropped/reordered/blocked output scenarios

## Acceptance Criteria
- forwarding behavior is deterministic under bursty and interleaved output
- tests cover nominal and failure conditions with reproducible artifacts
- logs clearly separate subprocess-origin issues from UI/runtime issues for fast triage.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:08.937970098Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:40.970633651Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","one-writer","subtask"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3gsu","depends_on_id":"wa-1uqi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:24:40.575896411Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-3gsu","depends_on_id":"wa-3cso","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:08.956003109Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}

 {"id":"wa-4df","title":"[Human command] `wa backup export` (database + config archive with checksum)","description":"# Task: wa backup export

## Goal
Create portable backup archives that users can store, copy, and restore from.

## Command Design
```bash
# Basic export
$ wa backup export
Backup saved: ~/.local/share/wa/backups/wa_backup_2026-01-18_143015.wa

# Custom output
$ wa backup export --output /path/to/backup.wa

# With compression
$ wa backup export --compress

# Include specific workspaces only
$ wa backup export --workspace /project/a --workspace /project/b

# Exclude large transcript data (config/events only)
$ wa backup export --metadata-only
```

## Output Format
Archive structure (tar + optional zstd):
```
backup.wa/
├── manifest.json      # Version, timestamp, checksums
├── schema_version     # Plain text: "3"
├── database.sql       # SQLite dump
├── config.toml        # Effective configuration
├── patterns/          # Custom pattern packs
│   └── custom.toml
└── checksums.sha256   # Per-file checksums
```

manifest.json:
```json
{
  "wa_version": "0.1.0",
  "schema_version": 3,
  "created_at": "2026-01-18T14:30:15Z",
  "workspaces": ["/project/a", "/project/b"],
  "stats": {
    "segments": 12345,
    "events": 567,
    "panes": 8
  },
  "checksum": "sha256:abc123..."
}
```

## Implementation Notes
- Use SQLite `.dump` for portability
- Acquire read lock during export (snapshot isolation)
- Stream large exports to avoid memory issues
- Verify checksum immediately after write

## Testing
- Unit tests: archive format validation, manifest generation
- Integration: export with active watcher, verify no corruption
- E2E: export + import round-trip preserves all data

## Acceptance Criteria
- Export creates valid, portable archive
- Checksum included and verified on write
- Progress shown for large databases
- Metadata-only mode for quick config backup

 {"id":"wa-4r7","title":"Multi-account exhaustion handling: all accounts at limit → graceful degradation","description":"# Multi-account exhaustion handling

## Purpose
Handle the scenario where ALL configured accounts have reached their usage limits simultaneously.

## Problem
Current usage limit workflow assumes at least one account is available for failover.
When all accounts are exhausted, we need graceful degradation:
1. Don't attempt impossible failovers
2. Provide clear status to user
3. Schedule auto-resume at earliest reset time
4. Support notification of this critical state

## Detection
```rust
// In account selection logic
let available = accounts.iter()
    .filter(|a| a.percent_remaining > 0.0 || a.reset_time < now)
    .collect::<Vec<_>>();

if available.is_empty() {
    return Err(AccountExhausted {
        next_reset: accounts.iter()
            .map(|a| a.reset_time)
            .min(),
        accounts_checked: accounts.len(),
    });
}
```

## Workflow Behavior
When `AccountExhausted` detected:

### Step 1: Capture state
- Save current session transcript
- Record in-progress task (if determinable)
- Log which accounts were checked

### Step 2: Calculate resume time
```rust
let earliest_reset = accounts.iter()
    .filter_map(|a| a.reset_time)
    .min();
```

### Step 3: Persist "awaiting reset" state
```sql
INSERT INTO workflow_paused 
(pane_id, reason, resume_at, state_snapshot)
VALUES (?, 'all_accounts_exhausted', ?, ?);
```

### Step 4: Notify user
```
🔴 All accounts exhausted

All 3 configured OpenAI accounts have reached their usage limits.

Next reset: account-2 @ 2026-01-19 00:00 UTC (6h 23m)

Options:
  1. Wait for reset (wa will auto-resume if enabled)
  2. Add new account: wa accounts add
  3. Continue manually in exhausted account (with limits)

Status: wa accounts status
Resume: wa workflow resume --when-available
```

### Step 5: Schedule resume (optional)
If auto-resume enabled:
- Create timer/cron for earliest reset time
- Resume workflow when triggered

## Configuration
```toml
[accounts.exhaustion]
notify_channels = ["desktop", "webhook"]
auto_resume_on_reset = true
save_transcript = true
```

## Robot Mode Error
```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "error": {
    "code": "E_ALL_ACCOUNTS_EXHAUSTED",
    "message": "All configured accounts have reached usage limits",
    "data": {
      "accounts_checked": 3,
      "next_reset": "2026-01-19T00:00:00Z",
      "next_reset_account": "account-2"
    }
  }
}
```

## Testing
- Unit: Account selection returns exhaustion error correctly
- Unit: Resume time calculation
- Integration: Workflow pauses correctly
- E2E: Full exhaustion → notification → resume flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Exhaustion detected when all accounts at limit
- [ ] Clear user notification with reset times
- [ ] State preserved for resume
- [ ] Auto-resume works when enabled
- [ ] Robot mode returns structured error","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:14:14.002558283Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T17:30:34.037935162Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T17:30:34.037786926Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-accounts","area-workflows","phase-2"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:25Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:25Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:25Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:25Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-c7o","title":"[EPIC] Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation","description":"# Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation

## Overview
This epic builds the workflow automation system that responds to detected patterns. The workflow engine is what makes wa "active" rather than just a passive monitor - it can take corrective actions when agents hit limits, experience compaction, need authentication, etc.

## Strategic Importance
The workflow system transforms wa from a "monitoring tool" into an "automation platform". This is where the real value emerges:
- Automatic context recovery after compaction
- Automatic account rotation on usage limits
- Automatic authentication renewal
- Foundation for more sophisticated multi-agent coordination

## Key Components

### 1. Workflow Engine Architecture
- Trait-based workflow definition: \`Workflow\` trait with name, description, handles, steps, execute_step
- Step results: Continue, Done, Retry, Abort, WaitFor
- WaitCondition: Pattern, PaneIdle, External
- Per-pane workflow locking (prevent conflicting workflows)
- Idempotent, recoverable, audited execution

### 2. Handle Usage Limits Workflow (Codex First)
Steps:
1. Exit current session gracefully (Ctrl-C twice for Codex)
2. Parse session info for resume (session ID, token usage)
3. Select next available account
4. Perform account authentication
5. Resume previous session
6. Send continue prompt

This is the most complex workflow and proves out the architecture.

### 3. Handle Compaction Workflow
Steps:
1. Wait for compaction to complete (stabilization window)
2. Send agent-specific context refresh prompt
   - Claude Code: "Reread AGENTS.md so it's still fresh in your mind."
   - Codex: "Please re-read AGENTS.md and any key project context files."
   - Gemini: "Please re-examine AGENTS.md and project context."

### 4. Browser Automation Skeleton
- Playwright integration structure
- Browser profile persistence
- OpenAI device auth flow (skeleton)
- Anthropic OAuth flow (skeleton)
- Google auth flow (skeleton)
- Semi-automated fallback (open browser for human when automation fails)

### 5. CAUT Integration
- Shell out to \`caut\` for usage tracking
- get_usage(service) - current usage for OpenAI/Anthropic/Google
- refresh_usage(service) - refresh from APIs
- Account selection logic based on percent_remaining

## Workflow Design Constraints
Every workflow must be:
- **Idempotent**: Re-running doesn't make a mess
- **Recoverable**: Can resume after a crash
- **Audited**: Every step recorded (what was observed, what was sent)
- **Guarded**: Each step validates state before action

## Workflow Execution Model
Each step follows: Observe → Decide → Act → Verify

Step can return:
- \`Continue\` - proceed to next step
- \`WaitFor(condition)\` - pause until condition met
- \`Retry(after)\` - retry same step after delay
- \`Abort(reason)\` - stop workflow with error
- \`Done(result)\` - workflow completed successfully

## Data-Driven Workflow Descriptors
In addition to compiled Rust workflows, support YAML/TOML descriptors for simple prompt injection sequences:

\`\`\`yaml
# ~/.config/wa/workflows/custom_compaction.yaml
name: custom_compaction_prompt
trigger:
  event: session.compaction
  agent: claude_code
steps:
  - wait_stable: 2s
  - send: |
      Please re-read AGENTS.md and the project's README.md.
  - wait_for: "❯"
\`\`\`

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Workflow engine can execute multi-step workflows
- [ ] handle_compaction workflow works end-to-end for Claude Code
- [ ] handle_usage_limits workflow works end-to-end for Codex (at least skeleton)
- [ ] Browser automation structure is in place (can be extended in Phase 3)
- [ ] CAUT integration returns account usage data
- [ ] Workflows are audited and recoverable

## Dependencies
- Depends on Phase 1 (Foundation) - needs pattern engine, storage, watcher

## Technical Notes

### Why Codex First for Usage Limits?
- Codex has the clearest device auth flow
- Resume mechanism is well-documented
- Good test case for the complexity

### Semi-Automated Browser Auth
If Playwright can't complete auth (MFA, CAPTCHA, password):
1. Open non-headless browser
2. Request human to complete auth
3. Persist browser profile for future sessions
4. Notify when auth complete

This provides graceful degradation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:47:54.895212760Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of wa-nu4.1 (Phase 2: Workflows). wa-nu4.1 has correct dependency chain. Content merged conceptually.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"wa-cjn","title":"E2E test scripts with detailed logging","description":"## Scripts to Create

### scripts/e2e_test.sh (Master)
Runs all E2E tests, aggregates results

### scripts/test_wezterm_interface.sh
- Test list_panes with running WezTerm
- Test get_text retrieval
- Test send_text delivery
- Verify error handling

### scripts/test_storage.sh
- Create temp DB
- Insert segments
- Verify FTS search
- Test gap recording

### scripts/test_ingest.sh
- Start watcher against mock pane
- Verify segment capture
- Test gap detection

### scripts/test_patterns.sh
- Run pattern engine against corpus
- Verify all detections match expected

## Logging Requirements
All scripts must:
1. Print "[TIMESTAMP] [TEST_NAME] Starting..."
2. Print "[TIMESTAMP] [TEST_NAME] PASS/FAIL: reason"
3. Save full logs to /tmp/wa_e2e_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.log
4. Support --verbose for extra output
5. Exit 0 if all pass, 1+ if any fail

## Acceptance
- `./scripts/e2e_test.sh` runs without error
- Clear pass/fail output
- Logs captured for debugging","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:56:27.719916382Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T08:57:09.519044012Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T08:57:09.519044012Z","close_reason":"Redundant - testing already covered by wa-4vx.10 and component test tasks","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-testing","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-cjn","depends_on_id":"wa-0je","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:32Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-dhd","title":"Structured logging infrastructure: JSON format, tracing spans, contextual fields","description":"# Structured Logging Infrastructure

## Purpose
Implement consistent structured logging across all wa components.

## Implementation

### Tracing Setup
```rust
use tracing_subscriber::{fmt, EnvFilter, layer::SubscriberExt};

pub fn init_logging(config: &LogConfig) -> Result<()> {
    let filter = EnvFilter::try_from_default_env()
        .unwrap_or_else(|_| EnvFilter::new(&config.log_level));
    
    let subscriber = tracing_subscriber::registry()
        .with(filter)
        .with(match config.log_format {
            LogFormat::Json => fmt::layer().json().boxed(),
            LogFormat::Pretty => fmt::layer().pretty().boxed(),
        });
    
    tracing::subscriber::set_global_default(subscriber)?;
    Ok(())
}
```

### Contextual Spans
```rust
#[tracing::instrument(
    skip(self, text),
    fields(pane_id = %pane_id, text_len = text.len())
)]
pub async fn send_text(&self, pane_id: u32, text: &str) -> Result<()> {
    // ... implementation
}
```

### Log Entry Structure
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-01-18T14:30:00.123Z",
  "level": "INFO",
  "target": "wa::ingest",
  "message": "Segment persisted",
  "pane_id": 3,
  "segment_id": 12345,
  "bytes": 1024,
  "duration_ms": 2.5
}
```

### Sensitive Data Redaction
```rust
impl Redactable for SendTextAction {
    fn redact(&self) -> Self {
        Self {
            text: redact_secrets(&self.text),
            ..self.clone()
        }
    }
}

fn redact_secrets(text: &str) -> String {
    // Patterns: API keys, tokens, passwords
    REDACTION_PATTERNS.iter()
        .fold(text.to_string(), |s, (pattern, replacement)| {
            pattern.replace_all(&s, *replacement).to_string()
        })
}
```

## Log Levels
- **trace**: Detailed internal operations (segment boundaries, pattern checks)
- **debug**: Diagnostic information (config loaded, connection established)
- **info**: Normal operations (workflow started, event detected)
- **warn**: Recoverable issues (retry needed, fallback used)
- **error**: Failures requiring attention (workflow failed, connection lost)

## Testing
- Unit: JSON format is valid, redaction works
- Integration: Log levels filter correctly
- E2E: Logs appear in expected format during operations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] tracing subscriber configured with JSON and pretty modes
- [ ] All public functions have appropriate spans
- [ ] Contextual fields (pane_id, workflow_id) propagate
- [ ] Sensitive data redacted from logs
- [ ] Log levels documented and consistent

 {"id":"wa-dug","title":"[EPIC] Smart Environment Detection: auto-configure based on detected setup","description":"# [EPIC] Smart Environment Detection

## Mission
Automatically detect the user's environment and configure wa optimally, reducing setup friction.

## Why This Matters
Users struggle with configuration:
- "Which settings do I need?"
- "Why isn't it detecting my agents?"
- "What's the right poll interval?"

Smart detection provides:
- **Zero-config start**: Works out of the box
- **Optimal defaults**: Tuned for detected environment
- **Guided setup**: Suggests improvements

## Scope

### Environment Detection
- WezTerm version and capabilities
- Shell type (bash, zsh, fish)
- OSC 133 support
- Agent presence and types
- Remote panes (SSH)
- System resources (CPU, RAM)

### Auto-configuration
- Poll intervals based on system load
- Pattern packs based on detected agents
- Storage paths based on OS conventions
- Safety defaults based on environment

### Detection API
```rust
pub struct DetectedEnvironment {
    wezterm: WeztermInfo,
    shell: ShellInfo,
    osc_133_support: bool,
    agents: Vec<DetectedAgent>,
    remotes: Vec<RemoteHost>,
    system: SystemInfo,
}
```

### CLI Integration
- `wa doctor` shows detection results
- `wa setup` uses detection for suggestions
- Config shows effective settings with sources

## Success Criteria
- New users need no configuration to start
- Detection is accurate for common setups
- Suggestions improve user experience

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Environment detection API complete
- [ ] Auto-configuration uses detection
- [ ] wa doctor shows detection results
- [ ] Suggestions are actionable
- [ ] Tests cover detection scenarios

## Testing
- Unit tests for probes and recommendation rules.
- Integration tests for doctor/setup output.
- E2E artifacts include detection results and rationale.

 {"id":"wa-dug.1","title":"Environment detection probes + API","description":"# Task: Environment detection probes + API

## Goal
Implement the **detected environment** API and probes used by setup/doctor/suggestions.

## Scope
- WezTerm detection:
  - version
  - mux server presence
  - pane count
- Shell detection:
  - bash/zsh/fish
  - OSC 133 support
- Agent detection:
  - known agent banners in recent output
- System resources:
  - CPU count
  - memory estimate

## Deliverables
- `DetectedEnvironment` struct + serialization
- Probe functions with bounded timeouts
- Cached detection results (avoid repeated slow checks)

## Testing
- Unit tests with mocked probes
- Integration tests with fixture outputs (wezterm list, shell markers)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Detection API returns consistent results and never blocks CLI for long.
- All probes have timeouts and actionable error messages.

 {"id":"wa-fno","title":"[EPIC] Plugin/Extension System: custom patterns, workflows, and integrations","description":"# [EPIC] Plugin/Extension System

## Mission
Enable users to extend wa with custom patterns, workflows, and integrations without modifying core code.

## Why This Matters
Every user has unique needs:
- Custom agent patterns not in built-in packs
- Proprietary workflow logic
- Integration with internal tools
- Organization-specific safety rules

A plugin system enables:
- **Customization** without forking
- **Sharing** of community extensions
- **Isolation** of custom code
- **Iteration** without core upgrades

## Core Insight: Extensibility as a Feature
The most successful developer tools are extensible (VS Code, Neovim, etc.). wa should be too.

## Scope

### Pattern Extensions
- User-defined pattern packs
- Custom extraction rules
- Pattern pack inheritance (extend built-in)

### Workflow Extensions
- Custom workflow definitions (YAML/TOML)
- Custom step implementations (Lua/WASM)
- Workflow hooks (pre/post)

### Integration Extensions
- Notification channels (Slack, Discord, custom)
- Export formatters
- Data sources

### Extension Management
- `wa ext install` / `wa ext list` / `wa ext remove`
- Local extensions directory
- Optional registry (community sharing)

## Success Criteria
- Users can add custom patterns without code changes
- Custom workflows run safely (sandboxed)
- Extensions are easy to share
- No security vulnerabilities from extensions

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests for extension loading
- Sandbox tests for untrusted code
- Integration tests for extension lifecycle
- Security tests for malicious extensions

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pattern packs loadable from user directory
- [ ] Custom workflows definable in YAML/TOML
- [ ] Extension management CLI works
- [ ] Extensions sandboxed (WASM or Lua)
- [ ] Documentation covers extension authoring
- [ ] Tests cover all extension types","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:32.094417025Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:32.094417025Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-extensibility","area-patterns","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-fno","depends_on_id":"wa-8bk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:33Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-fno.1","title":"User pattern packs: load custom rules from ~/.config/wa/patterns/","description":"# User pattern packs

## Purpose
Allow users to define custom pattern packs that load alongside built-in packs.

## User Pack Location
```
~/.config/wa/patterns/
├── my-org-patterns/
│   ├── pack.toml        # Pack metadata
│   └── rules.toml       # Rule definitions
└── custom-agent/
    ├── pack.toml
    └── rules.toml
```

## Pack Format
```toml
# pack.toml
[pack]
name = "my-org-patterns"
version = "1.0.0"
description = "Custom patterns for internal agents"
extends = "core.codex"  # Optional: inherit from built-in

# rules.toml
[[rules]]
id = "my-org.custom_warning"
name = "Custom Warning Pattern"
pattern = "\\[INTERNAL\\] Warning: (.+)"
severity = "warning"
extract = { message = "$1" }
agent_types = ["codex", "custom-agent"]
```

## Loading Priority
1. Built-in packs (highest priority)
2. User packs in order found
3. Workspace-local packs (.wa/patterns/)

For conflicts, later packs override earlier ones unless `extends` is used.

## Implementation
```rust
pub fn load_all_packs(config: &Config) -> Result<Vec<PatternPack>> {
    let mut packs = vec![];
    
    // Load built-in
    packs.extend(load_builtin_packs()?);
    
    // Load user packs
    let user_dir = config.patterns_dir();
    if user_dir.exists() {
        for entry in fs::read_dir(user_dir)? {
            let pack = PatternPack::load(entry?.path())?;
            packs.push(pack);
        }
    }
    
    // Load workspace packs
    if let Some(workspace_dir) = config.workspace_patterns_dir() {
        // Similar loading
    }
    
    // Resolve inheritance and conflicts
    resolve_pack_inheritance(&mut packs)?;
    
    Ok(packs)
}
```

## Validation
User packs are validated on load:
- Valid TOML syntax
- Required fields present
- Regex patterns compile
- No duplicate rule IDs (within pack)

## Testing
- Unit tests for pack loading
- Tests for inheritance resolution
- Tests for conflict handling
- Tests for invalid pack rejection

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User packs load from ~/.config/wa/patterns/
- [ ] Pack format documented
- [ ] Inheritance (extends) works
- [ ] Conflict resolution is predictable
- [ ] Invalid packs rejected with clear errors
- [ ] Tests cover all scenarios","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:46.618300818Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:46.618300818Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-extensibility","area-patterns","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-fno.1","depends_on_id":"wa-fno","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:33Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-fno.1","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:33Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-fno.2","title":"Custom workflows: YAML/TOML definitions with step sequences","description":"# Custom workflows

## Purpose
Allow users to define custom workflows in YAML/TOML without writing Rust code.

## Workflow Definition Format
```yaml
# ~/.config/wa/workflows/my_custom_workflow.yaml
name: my_custom_workflow
description: "Handle our custom agent's restart prompt"
triggers:
  - event_type: "my-org.restart_prompt"
    agent_types: ["custom-agent"]

steps:
  - name: wait_for_stable
    action: wait
    condition:
      type: pane_idle
      timeout: 5s
  
  - name: send_restart_command
    action: send_text
    text: "/restart\n"
    policy: require_approval  # or allow, deny
  
  - name: verify_restart
    action: wait
    condition:
      type: pattern
      pattern: "Agent restarted successfully"
      timeout: 30s

on_failure:
  action: notify
  message: "Custom workflow failed at step: ${failed_step}"
```

## Step Actions
```yaml
# Available actions
actions:
  - send_text      # Send keystrokes to pane
  - wait           # Wait for condition
  - notify         # Send notification
  - log            # Write to audit log
  - abort          # Stop workflow
  - conditional    # If/else branching
  - loop           # Repeat steps
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct YamlWorkflow {
    name: String,
    description: String,
    triggers: Vec<WorkflowTrigger>,
    steps: Vec<WorkflowStep>,
    on_failure: Option<FailureHandler>,
}

impl Workflow for YamlWorkflow {
    async fn execute(&self, ctx: &mut WorkflowContext) -> Result<WorkflowResult> {
        for step in &self.steps {
            match self.execute_step(step, ctx).await {
                Ok(StepResult::Continue) => continue,
                Ok(StepResult::Abort) => break,
                Err(e) => {
                    if let Some(handler) = &self.on_failure {
                        handler.execute(ctx, &e).await?;
                    }
                    return Err(e);
                }
            }
        }
        Ok(WorkflowResult::Success)
    }
}
```

## Testing
- Unit tests for YAML parsing
- Step action tests
- Conditional and loop tests
- Failure handling tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] YAML format supports all required actions
- [ ] Workflows load from user directory
- [ ] Steps execute in sequence
- [ ] Conditions work correctly
- [ ] Failure handlers execute
- [ ] Documentation covers workflow authoring
- [ ] Tests cover all step types","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"JadeFox","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:55:02.320177158Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-08T09:16:18.977157107Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T09:16:18.977080405Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-extensibility","area-workflows","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-fno.2","depends_on_id":"wa-fno","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:33Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-fno.2","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:33Z","created_by":"import"}]}
